name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
الٓر ۚ كِتَـٰبٌ أُحْكِمَتْ ءَايَـٰتُهُۥ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِن لَّدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ (١)
```

```english
Alif, Lam Ra. [This is] a Book, whose signs have been made definitive and then elaborated, from One [who is] all-wise, all-aware, (1)
```

```arabic
أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوٓا۟ إِلَّا ٱللَّهَ ۚ إِنَّنِى لَكُم مِّنْهُ نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ (٢)
```

```english
declaring: ‘Worship no one but Allah. I am indeed a warner to you from Him and a bearer of good news. (2)
```

```arabic
وَأَنِ ٱسْتَغْفِرُوا۟ رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوٓا۟ إِلَيْهِ يُمَتِّعْكُم مَّتَـٰعًا حَسَنًا إِلَىٰٓ أَجَلٍ مُّسَمًّى وَيُؤْتِ كُلَّ ذِى فَضْلٍ فَضْلَهُۥ ۖ وَإِن تَوَلَّوْا۟ فَإِنِّىٓ أَخَافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذَابَ يَوْمٍ كَبِيرٍ (٣)
```

```english
Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently. He will provide you with a good provision for a specified term and grant His grace to every meritorious person. But if you turn your backs [on Him], indeed I fear for you the punishment of a terrible day. (3)
```

```arabic
إِلَى ٱللَّهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ ۖ وَهُوَ عَلَىٰ كُلِّ شَىْءٍ قَدِيرٌ (٤)
```

```english
To Allah will be your return, and He has power over all things.’ (4)
```

## (Commentary)

The Surah, as is evident from its opening and conclusion and the context in which its verses flow, clarifies the purpose of the Quranic verses despite their abundance and diversity, and describes the sum of their objectives notwithstanding their variety and the summary of their contents.

It mentions that, while containing various religious sciences—from the principles of divine knowledge (أصول المعارف الإلهية) and noble human ethics (الأخلاق الكريمة الإنسانية) to the legal rulings (الأحكام الشرعية) pertaining to the universals of worship (العبادات), transactions (المعاملات), politics (السياسات), and governance (الولايات)—it then provides a description of the general creation, such as the Throne (العرش), the Pedestal (الكرسي), the Tablet (اللوح), the Pen (القلم), the heavens, the earth, the angels, the jinn, the devils, plants, animals, and man. It also describes the beginning of creation and that to which it shall return in terms of annihilation and the return to Allah, the Glorified.

This is the Day of Resurrection (يوم البعث), preceded by the world of the grave—which is the intermediate realm (البرزخ) — *the state between death and resurrection* — then the standing before the Lord of all the worlds (رب العالمين), the gathering (الحشر), the assembly (الجمع), the questioning (السؤال), the reckoning (الحساب), the weighing (الوزن), and the testimony of the witnesses (شهادة الأشهاد); then the final judgment (فصل القضاء), and then Paradise or the Fire, with their respective degrees (الدرجات) and depths (الدركات).

Furthermore, it describes the link between the creation of man and his actions, and between his actions and what follows them of felicity (سعادة) or wretchedness (شقاوة), blessing (نعمة) or vengeance (نقمة), and degree or depth. It also addresses what pertains to that of promise (الوعد) and threat (الوعيد), warning (الإنذار) and glad tidings (التبشير) through exhortation (الموعظة), graceful disputation (المجادلة الحسنة), and wisdom (الحكمة).

Thus, the Quranic verses, despite containing the details of these divine sciences and true realities, rely upon a single reality which is the root (الأصل), while these are its branches. It is the foundation upon which the structure of religion is built, namely His monotheism (التوحيد) — *the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts* — the monotheism of Islam (توحيد الإسلام), by believing that He, the Exalted, is the Lord of everything, there is no lord besides Him, and by submitting to Him from every direction, thereby fulfilling the right of His Lordship (الربوبية), such that the heart feels no awe and the action shows no submission except to Him, majestic is His affair (جل أمره).

This is a principle to which all the details of the Quranic meanings—from its sciences and laws—return through analysis (التحليل), and it returns to them, in all their detail, through synthesis (التركيب).

The Surah clarifies this in a summary fashion in these four verses with which it opens, then proceeds to its detailed explanation with the mark (بسمة) of warning and glad tidings by mentioning the established practice (السنة الجارية) of Allah among His servants. it cites the reports of past nations and the stories of the peoples of Nuh, Hud, Salih, Lut, Shu’ayb, and Musa (عليه السلام), and what they were led to by their arrogance (الاستكبار) in refusing to respond to the divine call, their corruption (الإفساد) in the land, and their excess (الإسراف) in their affairs. It describes what Allah promised those who believe and perform righteous deeds (الصالحات) and what Allah threatened those who disbelieve and deny the signs (الآيات). Amidst this, it clarifies matters of divine knowledge pertaining to monotheism (التوحيد), prophethood (النبوة), and the hereafter (المعاد).

From what has preceded, the error in the statement of some becomes apparent when they mentioned the purpose of this Surah: that it is similar in meaning and subject to Surah Yunus, which is the fundamental doctrines of Islam regarding divinity, prophethood, resurrection, and recompense, and the performance of righteous deeds, and that it details what was summarized in Surah Yunus regarding the stories of the messengers (عليه السلام). [End of quote].

You have already realized that the two Surahs are directed toward two different purposes, neither of which returns to the other at all. Surah Yunus clarifies that the divine practice (السنة الإلهية) is established upon the judgment (القضاء) between the messengers and their nations who denied them, then it warns this nation with what occurred to those before them. Surah Hud, however, clarifies that the Quranic sciences return through analysis to pure monotheism (التوحيد الخالص), just as monotheism returns, according to synthesis, to the details of the primary and secondary sciences.

The Surah—as its verses testify by their contents and the apparent connection between them—is Meccan, revealed all at once. It has been narrated from some that they excepted His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَعَلَّكَ تارِكٌ بَعْضَ ما يُوحى إِلَيْكَ** (12), mentioning that it is Medinan.

Some also excepted His saying: **أَفَمَنْ كانَ عَلى بَيِّنَةٍ مِنْ رَبِّهِ** (17), and others His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَقِمِ الصَّلاةَ طَرَفَيِ النَّهارِ وَزُلَفاً مِنَ اللَّيْلِ** (114). However, there is no evidence for any of that from the perspective of the wording (اللفظ), and the apparent continuity of the verses indicates that they are all Meccan.

His saying, the Exalted: **الر كِتابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آياتُهُ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِنْ لَدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ** — *Alif, Lam, Ra. [This is] a Book whose signs have been made definitive and then elaborated from One who is all-wise, all-aware.* (1) The contrast between "making definitive" (الإحكام) and "elaborating" (التفصيل)—which is the creation of a separation (الفصل) between the parts of a thing that are connected to one another, and the differentiation between matters that are merged into each other—indicates that what is meant by "making definitive" is the linking of one part of a thing to another and the returning of one side of it to another, such that the whole becomes a single, simple (بسيط) thing, devoid of parts and portions.

It is well known that when a book is described as being "made definitive" and "elaborated" in this sense, it is only so described in terms of the meaning (المعنى) and content (المضمون) it contains, not in terms of its words (الألفاظ) or anything else. The state of meanings in terms of definitiveness, elaboration, unity, and diversity is different from the state of concrete entities (الأعيان). When multiple meanings return to a single meaning, this single one is the preserved root (الأصل المحفوظ) in all of them; it is precisely these details in its summary form, and they are precisely that summary in their detailed form. All of this is apparent and beyond doubt.

Accordingly, the fact that the verses of the Book are "made definitive" first and then "elaborated" second means that the noble Quranic verses, despite their diverse contents and scattered objectives and purposes, return to a single, simple meaning and a unique, primary purpose in which there is no multiplicity or scattering. This is such that no noble verse seeks any objective or aims at any goal except that the primary purpose is the spirit (الروح) permeating its body and the reality sought from it.

Thus, this noble Book, despite the scattering of its verses and the separation of its parts, has but one unified purpose. When elaborated, it becomes a religious principle (أصل ديني) in one instance, an ethical matter (أمر خلقي) in another, and a legal ruling (حكم شرعي) in a third, and so on. Whenever it descends from the principles to their branches, and from the branches to the sub-branches, it does not exit its single, preserved meaning, nor does it miss its purpose. This single root, through its synthesis, becomes every single part of the detailed doctrines, ethics, and actions; and they, through their analysis and return to the permeating spirit that governs their bodies, return to that single root.

For example, His monotheism (توحيده), in a manner befitting the presence of His might and majesty, is, in the station of belief (الاعتقاد), the affirmation of His Beautiful Names (الأسماء الحسنى) and Supreme Attributes (الصفات العليا). In the station of ethics (الأخلاق), it is the adoption of noble traits such as contentment (الرضا), submission (التسليم), courage (الشجاعة), chastity (العفة), and generosity (السخاء), and the avoidance of base qualities. In the station of actions and deeds (الأعمال والأفعال), it is the performance of righteous deeds and piety (الورع) regarding the prohibitions of Allah.

If you wish, you could say: pure monotheism (التوحيد الخالص) necessitates in each of the levels of belief, ethics, and actions that which the Divine Book clarifies, just as each of these levels, as well as their parts, is not complete without pure monotheism.

It has thus become clear that the verse is in the station of clarifying the return of the details of Quranic sciences and laws to a single root. This root is such that if it is synthesized in every instance of belief, attribute, and action with the specificity of that instance, it produces a ruling specific to it from the Quranic rulings. By this, it becomes clear:

First: That His saying: **كِتابٌ** (a Book) is the predicate (خبر) of a suppressed subject (مبتدأ), the estimation being: "This is a Book." What is meant by the Book is what is in our hands of the Quran, divided into Surahs and verses. This does not contradict what is sometimes mentioned that the Book refers to the Preserved Tablet (اللوح المحفوظ) or the Quran as it is in the Tablet, for this recited Book is united with what is in the Tablet, a union of the revelation (التنزيل) with the esoteric interpretation (التأويل).

Second: That the word **ثُمَ** (then) in His saying: **ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ** (then elaborated) is to indicate a sequence in the order of discourse (التراخي بحسب ترتيب الكلام) rather than a temporal sequence (التراخي الزماني), as there is no meaning for temporal precedence or delay between meanings that differ in terms of being primary and secondary, or summary and detailed.

It also becomes clear what is problematic in some of what the masters of exegesis have mentioned regarding the meaning of the verse, such as the statement of some: that its meaning is that its verses were made definitive so that none of them were abrogated as previous books and laws were abrogated, then they were elaborated by the clarification of the lawful (الحلال), the prohibited (الحرام), and other rulings.

The flaw in this is: that according to this meaning, it would be necessary to restrict the lack of abrogation to the lack of abrogation by a book other than the Quran that would abrogate the Quran after it, just as the Quran abrogated others. For the existence of abrogation (النسخ) among the Quranic verses themselves is something that should not be doubted. Yet, the aforementioned restriction has no indication from the wording of the verse.

And like the statement of some: that the meaning is that its verses were made definitive by command and prohibition, then elaborated by promise and threat, reward and punishment. The flaw here is that it is an arbitrary claim (تحكم) for which there is no evidence at all.

And like the statement of some: that the meaning is the making definitive of its wording by placing it upon the most eloquent forms of rhetoric until it became a miracle (معجز), and its elaboration through explanation and clarification. The critique of this view is the same as the previous one.

And like the statement of some: the meaning of making its verses definitive is making them firm and solid (متقنة) with no defect or falsehood in them, and the meaning of their elaboration is making them follow one after another. The flaw is: that elaboration (التفصيل) in this sense is not linguistically recognized, unless it is interpreted to mean differentiation and multiplication, in which case it returns to the meaning we previously presented.

And like the statement of some: that the meaning is that its verses were made definitive as a whole, then separated in revelation verse by verse so that the person bound by religious law (المكلف) would be better able to reflect and ponder.

The flaw in this is: that this view is more appropriately mentioned regarding verses such as His saying, the Exalted: **إِنَّا أَنْزَلْناهُ فِي لَيْلَةٍ مُبارَكَةٍ** — *Indeed We sent it down on a blessed night* (Ad-Dukhan: 3), and His saying: **وَقُرْآناً فَرَقْناهُ لِتَقْرَأَهُ عَلَى النَّاسِ عَلى مُكْثٍ وَنَزَّلْناهُ تَنْزِيلاً** — *A Quran which We have unfolded gradually that you may recite it to the people at intervals, and We have sent it down in stages.* (Al-Isra: 106), and similar verses which indicate that the Quran has a level with Allah that is higher than the surface of understanding, then it descended to a level that accepts comprehension and reflection out of consideration for the state of ordinary understandings, as is also indicated by His saying: **وَالْكِتابِ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ وَإِنَّهُ فِي أُمِّ الْكِتابِ لَدَيْنا لَعَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ** — *By the Manifest Book! Indeed We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason. And indeed it is with Us in the Mother of the Book, sublime and wise.* (Az-Zukhruf: 2-4).

As for our verse here: **كِتابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آياتُهُ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ**, He has linked both the making definitive and the elaboration to the verses (الآيات). This can only be from the perspective of their meanings. Thus, it conveys that the definitiveness and elaboration are in the meanings of these multiple verses; they have a dimension of unity and simplicity, and a dimension of multiplicity and complexity. This corresponds to the meaning we presented, not to what was mentioned which pertains to the issue of esoteric interpretation (التأويل) and revelation (التنزيل); so understand that.

And like the statement of some: that what is meant by definitiveness and elaboration is the summarization (إجمال) of some verses and the clarification (تبيين) of others. They have exemplified this with His saying, the Exalted, in this Surah: **مَثَلُ الْفَرِيقَيْنِ كَالْأَعْمى وَالْأَصَمِّ وَالْبَصِيرِ وَالسَّمِيعِ** — *The parable of the two parties is that of the blind and the deaf, and the seeing and the hearing.* (24), for it is a summary definitive verse that is clarified by what appears in the Surah of the stories of Nuh, Hud, and Salih, and so on.

The flaw in this is: that the apparent meaning of the verse is that the definitiveness and elaboration are united in terms of their subject, meaning that the very verses upon which the definitiveness occurred are the ones upon which the elaboration occurred. It is not that definitiveness is a description of some of its verses and elaboration is a description of others, as is necessitated by what they mentioned.

**And His saying, the Exalted:** **مِنْ لَدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ** — *from One who is all-wise, all-aware.* (1). The All-Wise (الحكيم) is one of His active Beautiful Names, indicating the perfection of craftsmanship (إتقان الصنع). Likewise, the All-Aware (الخبير) is one of His Beautiful Names indicating His knowledge of the particulars of the states of existing things and their benefits. The attribution of making the verses definitive and elaborating them to His being All-Wise and All-Aware is due to the relationship between them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللهَ إِنَّنِي لَكُمْ مِنْهُ نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ وَأَنِ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ** — *‘[Saying,] “Worship no one but Allah. Indeed I am a warner to you from Him and a bearer of good news. And [saying,] ‘Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently...’* (2-3). This verse and what follows it are an explanation (تفسير) of the content of the first verse: **كِتابٌ أُحْكِمَتْ آياتُهُ ثُمَّ فُصِّلَتْ مِنْ لَدُنْ حَكِيمٍ خَبِيرٍ**. Since the verse implies that it is a Book from Allah to... having verses made definitive then elaborated, the concern in its explanation is directed toward clarifying these aspects.

It is well known that this Book, which Allah the Exalted sent down from Himself to His Messenger to recite to the people and convey to them, has an aspect of address to the Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) and an aspect of address to the people through his mediation. As for the aspect of address to the Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—which is what the Messenger receives from the revelation of Allah—it is: "Warn, give glad tidings, and call the people to such and such." This aspect is what was intended at the beginning of Surah Yunus where He, the Exalted, said: **أَوْحَيْنا إِلى رَجُلٍ مِنْهُمْ أَنْ أَنْذِرِ النَّاسَ وَبَشِّرِ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا أَنَّ لَهُمْ قَدَمَ صِدْقٍ عِنْدَ رَبِّهِمْ** — *We revealed to a man from among them, [saying], ‘Warn the people, and give good news to the faithful that they have a standing of truth with their Lord’* (Yunus: 2).

As for the aspect of address to the people—which is what the people receive from the Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم)—it is what he conveys to the people of the meaning within his recitation of the speech of Allah to them by way of his messengership: "I call you to Allah with the call of a warner and a bearer of glad tidings." This aspect of address is what was intended in His saying: **أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللهَ إِنَّنِي لَكُمْ مِنْهُ نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ**.

Thus, the verse is from the speech of Allah, explaining the meaning of making the verses of the Book definitive then elaborating them, by narrating what the people receive of the Messenger's call to them through the recitation of the Book of Allah. It is not the speech of the Messenger by way of narration, nor by the estimation of "say" (قول), nor is it a case of rhetorical transition (الالتفات). Nor is the estimation: "He commanded you that you worship not," or: "Its verses were elaborated so that you worship not except Allah," such that His saying: **أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا** would be a negation (نفي) rather than a prohibition (نهي). For His subsequent saying: **وَأَنِ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ** is conjoined to His saying: **أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللهَ**, which testifies that **أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا** is a prohibition, not a negation. Furthermore, estimation is not resorted to without evidence; so understand that, for it is among the subtle crafts of eloquence in the verse.

Accordingly, His saying: **أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللهَ** is a call to the monotheism of worship (توحيد العبادة) by prohibiting the worship of other than Allah from the deities taken as partners to Allah, and restricting worship to Him, the Exalted. His saying: **وَأَنِ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ** is a command to seek forgiveness from Allah—having taken Him as their Lord by rejecting the worship of others—then a command to repent and return to Him through righteous deeds. The result of the whole is the traversing of the natural path that leads to proximity and nearness (الزلفى) to Him, the Exalted: which is the rejection of deities besides Allah, then seeking forgiveness and spiritual purity (الطهارة النفسانية) to be present in the enclosure of proximity, then returning to Him, the Exalted, through righteous deeds.

The explanatory particle "that" (أن التفسيرية) was brought a second time in His saying: **وَأَنِ اسْتَغْفِرُوا** due to the difference between the two stages indicated by His saying: **أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللهَ**—which is the stage of monotheism through sincere worship—and His saying: **وَأَنِ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ**—which is the stage of righteous action, even if the second is among the results and branches of the first.

Because monotheism is the fundamental root and seeking forgiveness and repentance are a result and a branch stemming from it, He mentioned the warning and glad tidings after mentioning monotheism, and the beautiful promise contained in His saying: **يُمَتِّعْكُمْ** (He will provide you) after mentioning seeking forgiveness and repentance. Thus He said: **أَلَّا تَعْبُدُوا إِلَّا اللهَ إِنَّنِي لَكُمْ مِنْهُ نَذِيرٌ وَبَشِيرٌ**, clarifying thereby that the warning and glad tidings, whatever they may be, return to monotheism and pertain to it. Then He said: **وَأَنِ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ يُمَتِّعْكُمْ مَتاعاً حَسَناً**, for valuable effects and sought-after good results only follow a thing after it is complete in itself and perfected with its attributes, branches, and results. Monotheism, though it is the sole root of religion in its vastness, its tree does not bear fruit until it stands upon its trunk and its branches and boughs spread out: **كَلِمَةً طَيِّبَةً كَشَجَرَةٍ طَيِّبَةٍ أَصْلُها ثابِتٌ وَفَرْعُها فِي السَّماءِ تُؤْتِي أُكُلَها كُلَّ حِينٍ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّها** — *a good word is like a good tree: its roots are steady and its branches are in the sky. It gives its fruit every season by the leave of its Lord.* (Ibrahim: 24-25).

It appears that what is meant by repentance (التوبة) in the verse is faith (الإيمان), as in His saying, the Exalted: **فَاغْفِرْ لِلَّذِينَ تابُوا وَاتَّبَعُوا سَبِيلَكَ** — *so forgive those who repent and follow Your way* (Al-Mu'min: 7). Thus, the combination of seeking forgiveness and repentance, with repentance conjoined to it by "then" (ثم), becomes sound. The meaning is: leave the worship of idols after this, and seek from your Lord the forgiveness of the sins you have previously committed, then believe in your Lord.

It has been said: the meaning is seek forgiveness and make it your goal, then reach it through repentance; but this is not good. Among the forced interpretations (التكلف) is what some mentioned: that the meaning is: seek forgiveness for your past sins, then repent to Him whenever you sin in the future. Likewise is the statement of another: that **ثُمَ** in the verse has the meaning of "and" (الواو) because repentance and seeking forgiveness are one.

His saying: **يُمَتِّعْكُمْ مَتاعاً حَسَناً إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** — *...He will provide you with a good provision until a specified time* (3). The specified time (الأجل المسمى) is the time at which life ends, which it never exceeds. Thus, what is meant is the provision in the life of this world, or rather, with the life of this world, because Allah, the Glorified, has called it "provision" (متاع) in several places of His speech. Thus, the "good provision until a specified time" is nothing other than the good worldly life.

The meaning of His saying: **يُمَتِّعْكُمْ مَتاعاً حَسَناً**—on the estimation that "provision" (متاعاً) is a cognate accusative (مفعول مطلق)—amounts to something like our saying: "He will provide you with a good provision through a good worldly life." The provision of life is only "good" if it leads man to his possible felicity and guides him to human aspirations of enjoying the blessings of the world in abundance, security, prosperity, might, and nobility. This good life stands in contrast to the "wretched life" (المعيشة الضنك) referred to in His saying: **وَمَنْ أَعْرَضَ عَنْ ذِكْرِي فَإِنَّ لَهُ مَعِيشَةً ضَنْكاً** — *But whoever disregards My remembrance, his shall be a wretched life* (Taha: 124).

There is no goodness in the provision of the worldly life, nor any abundance in living, for one who turns away from the remembrance of Allah and does not believe in his Lord. For although some people may be given an abundance of wealth and high status in the land, and then imagine that they have been given every human aspiration, they are heedless of the joy of one who has realized the reality of faith in Allah and entered into the guardianship (ولاية) of Allah. Such a person is given by Allah the good human life (الحياة الطيبة) and is secured from the humiliation of the animalistic life, in which there is no governance except for greed, gluttony, predation, dog-like rapacity, and ignorance. The free human soul censures that part of life which base and vile souls prefer, even if it entails humiliation, misery, and every vileness.

Thus, the good life for a righteous and free society is that they share in the enjoyment of the advantages of the earthly blessings that Allah created for them—a sharing based on mutual mercy, cooperation, and support, without transgression or crowding, such that each seeks his own good and benefit within the good and benefit of his society, without worshipping himself or enslaving others.

In short, enjoying the good life until a specified time is the individual's enjoyment of life according to what the human primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — deems good, which is moderation in material enjoyments in the light of beneficial knowledge and righteous action. This is when attributed to the individual. As for when it is attributed to society, it is the general benefit from the good blessings of earthly life by allocating what individuals attain through their toil and effort to the society whose parts are harmonized, without opposition between its portions or contradiction.

His saying: **وَيُؤْتِ كُلَّ ذِي فَضْلٍ فَضْلَهُ** — *...and give every meritorious person his reward.* (3). Merit (الفضل) is an increase or excellence. Since merit in His saying: **كُلَّ ذِي فَضْلٍ** (every possessor of merit) is attributed to the individuals who possess it, this serves as a contextual clue that the pronoun in **فَضْلَهُ** (his merit/reward) refers back to the possessor of merit, rather than to the Majestic Name (Allah), as some have conjectured. Merit and increase are relative meanings that are only realized by measuring one thing against another and relating it to it.

The meaning is: He gives everyone who has exceeded others in any of his attributes or actions—and what that necessitates of being singled out for increased and specific reward—the gift of felicity corresponding to that increase, without his right being nullified, his merit being usurped, or another being made its owner, as is observed in non-religious societies, even if they are civilly advanced. For humanity, since it inhabited the earth and formed various types of societies—savage, advanced, or even more advanced—has not ceased to be divided into two groups: a dominant, arrogant, and overpowering group, and a humiliated, enslaved, and overpowered group. Nothing balances this excess and deficiency, nor levels this disparity, except the religion of monotheism (دين التوحيد).

The religion of monotheism is the only system (السنة) that restricts mastership and sovereignty to Allah, the Glorified, and equates the strong and the weak, the foremost and the latecomer, the great and the small, the white and the black, the man and the woman. It proclaims the likes of His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى وَجَعَلْناكُمْ شُعُوباً وَقَبائِلَ لِتَعارَفُوا إِنَّ أَكْرَمَكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ أَتْقاكُمْ** — *O mankind! Indeed We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes that you may identify yourselves with one another. Indeed the noblest of you in the sight of Allah is the most God-conscious among you.* (Al-Hujurat: 13), and His saying: **أَنِّي لا أُضِيعُ عَمَلَ عامِلٍ مِنْكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ أَوْ أُنْثى بَعْضُكُمْ مِنْ بَعْضٍ** — *‘I do not leave the work of any worker to go to waste from among you, whether male or female; you are all on the same footing.’* (Al-Imran: 195).

Furthermore, the occurrence of His saying: **وَيُؤْتِ كُلَّ ذِي فَضْلٍ فَضْلَهُ**, which speaks of the regard for the merit of every meritorious person, after His saying: **يُمَتِّعْكُمْ مَتاعاً حَسَناً إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى**, which indicates the provision for everyone, suggests:

First: That what is meant by the first sentence is the general provision shared among the members of society—in other words, the general good life of the society—and by the second sentence, the advantages given to some individuals in exchange for the merit they specifically possess.

Second: That the first sentence refers to the provision of the worldly life, and the second to the giving of the reward of the hereafter in exchange for the righteous deeds performed by the individual; or the giving to every meritorious person his merit in both this world and the hereafter together, by singling out everyone who brought an increase in a worldly aspect with what his increase necessitates of advantage in the aspects of life. This is done by placing every possessor of virtue in a quality or action in the position necessitated by his quality or action, and putting him in his proper place, without equating the virtuous and the less virtuous in their religion, and without the specificities being removed or the degrees and ranks between social actions and efforts being nullified. Thus, the state of the one who is energetic in his work and the lazy one does not become the same, nor does the matter of the one who strives in precise and important work differ from the one who toys with trivial and easy work, and so on.

His saying: **وَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَإِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ كَبِيرٍ** — *But if you turn your backs, then I fear for you the punishment of a terrible day.* (3). That is, "if you (plural) turn away," using the second-person address. The evidence for this is His saying: **عَلَيْكُمْ** (for you) and the multiple second-person addresses that preceded in the two verses. Therefore, no attention should be paid to the statement of one who takes His saying: **تَوَلَّوْا** as a third-person masculine plural of the past tense, for its falsity is apparent.

One of the commentators has made a strange claim regarding His saying, the Exalted: **يُمَتِّعْكُمْ مَتاعاً حَسَناً إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى**, saying: "The verse implies the salvation of this Muhammadan nation from the punishment of extirpation (عذاب الاستئصال), as we also clarified in the exegesis of Surah Yunus." [End of quote]. I do not know how he derived what he mentioned from the verse. Perhaps he based it on the idea that the verse made the good life without extirpation conditional for the nation upon their believing in Allah and His signs, and then they believed and Islam spread in the world. However, it is well known that the Messenger (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) was sent to the people of the world in general, and the generality of them did not believe in him; nor did those who believed in him all purify their faith from hypocrisy, such that faith traveled from their outward to their inward and from their tongues to their hearts.

If the mere faith of some of the nation along with the disbelief of others were sufficient for the fulfillment of the condition and the removal of the punishment of extirpation, it would have been sufficient for the nations of Nuh, Hud (عليه السلام), and others. They called their nations to what Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) called to, and they stipulated for them the likes of what was stipulated for his nation, yet Allah encompassed them with the punishment of extirpation, and it was a duty upon Him to help the believers.

Allah, the Glorified, has narrated from Nuh his saying to his people within his call: **اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ إِنَّهُ كانَ غَفَّاراً يُرْسِلِ السَّماءَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِدْراراً وَيُمْدِدْكُمْ بِأَمْوالٍ وَبَنِينَ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ جَنَّاتٍ وَيَجْعَلْ لَكُمْ أَنْهاراً** — *‘Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, for He is all-forgiver: He will send for you abundant rains from the sky, and aid you with wealth and sons, and provide you with gardens and provide you with streams.’* (Nuh: 10-12). He narrated from Hud his saying: **وَيا قَوْمِ اسْتَغْفِرُوا رَبَّكُمْ ثُمَّ تُوبُوا إِلَيْهِ يُرْسِلِ السَّماءَ عَلَيْكُمْ مِدْراراً وَيَزِدْكُمْ قُوَّةً إِلى قُوَّتِكُمْ وَلا تَتَوَلَّوْا مُجرمِينَ** — *‘O my people! Plead with your Lord for forgiveness, then turn to Him penitently: He will send you abundant rains from the sky, and add power to your power, and do not turn away as guilty ones.’* (Hud: 52). And He narrated collectively from Nuh, Hud, Salih, and those after them their saying: **أَفِي اللهِ شَكٌّ فاطِرِ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ يَدْعُوكُمْ لِيَغْفِرَ لَكُمْ مِنْ ذُنُوبِكُمْ وَيُؤَخِّرَكُمْ إِلى أَجَلٍ مُسَمًّى** — *‘Is there any doubt about Allah, the originator of the heavens and the earth?! He calls you to forgive you some of your sins and to grant you respite until a specified time.’* (Ibrahim: 10).

As for his saying: "And we have clarified it in Surah Yunus as well," he brought nothing there but a hollow claim. We have already presented there that the verses of Surah Yunus are explicit in that Allah will judge between this nation and its Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم), punishing them and saving the believers—the practice of Allah (سنة الله) which has passed among His servants, and you will never find any change in the practice of Allah.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِلَى اللهِ مَرْجِعُكُمْ وَهُوَ عَلى كُلِّ شَيْءٍ قَدِيرٌ** — *To Allah is your return, and He has power over all things.* (4). This is in the station of providing the reason (التعليل) for what His saying: **وَإِنْ تَوَلَّوْا فَإِنِّي أَخافُ عَلَيْكُمْ عَذابَ يَوْمٍ كَبِيرٍ** implies regarding the return (المعاد). The end of the verse is directed toward removing what might occur in their hearts of deeming the resurrection unlikely after the occurrence of death. The meaning is: if you turn away from sincere worship of Him and the rejection of partners, then I fear for you the punishment of a terrible day that will meet you and you will face it; it is the Day of Resurrection after death, because your return is to Allah, and Allah has power over all things. Thus, He is not incapable of bringing you to life after causing death; so beware of deeming that unlikely.

The verse is thus a contextual clue that what is meant by the "terrible day" is the Day of Resurrection. Al-Qummi narrated in his exegesis, as a report from an Imam (مضمراً), that what is meant by the punishment of a terrible day is the Smoke (الدخان) and the Cry (الصيحة).
